SkillOpt","plain_text":"Models Are Not the Whole Story
Everyone is focused on models.
But the next major frontier in AI agents may actually be skills and rules.
I just read the SkillOpt paper from researchers working on self-evolving agent behavior, and it strongly validates something we’ve been seeing while building Blume:
The future isn’t just better LLMs.
It’s persistent context, executable memory, reusable agent skills, and systems that continuously optimize how agents operate.
From Static Prompts to Trainable Skills
 
The core idea in the paper is deceptively simple:
Treat agent “skills” as trainable artifacts instead of static prompts.
Agents execute tasks, outcomes are evaluated, skills are refined iteratively, and only improvements survive. It’s essentially gradient descent for agent behavior — but in text space.
That matters because autonomous workflows and multi-agent systems are rapidly becoming the interface to software development itself.
The New Optimization Layer
The shift happening right now is important:
We used to optimize code, infrastructure, and models.
Now we’re optimizing context, memory, workflows, instructions, and execution strategies.
And the reason is simple: teams are hitting diminishing returns with coding agents because the bottleneck is no longer raw model capability. It’s the behavioral substrate around the model — the rules, skills, context, and memory that make work reproducible.
That’s a fundamentally new software layer.
At Blume, we’ve been thinking about this transition as:
“AI generating code” → “AI-native software systems.”
And AI-native systems require durable operational memory, structured context, executable documentation, environment awareness, and reusable behavioral patterns.
Why Reliability Becomes the Hard Problem
One thing I especially liked in the paper is that it introduces optimization concepts analogous to ML training itself: bounded edit budgets, validation gates, rejected-edit buffers, and gradual updates.
That’s critical because agent behavior becomes extremely unstable without constraints.
 
Anyone building serious coding agents already knows the hard problem isn’t getting agents to succeed once — it’s reproducibility, controllability, and reliability across environments.
This is also why context engineering is becoming more important than prompt engineering.
Systems like AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, executable docs, workflow memory, and structured skill registries are quickly emerging as core infrastructure.
Operational Knowledge Becomes the Moat
The most interesting implication may be this:
Organizations could eventually accumulate proprietary corpora of skills, docs, workflows, and rules that become more valuable than the underlying models themselves.
Because models are commoditizing.
Operational knowledge compounds.
From “Using Tools” to “Operating Environments”
That’s also why this wave of coding agents feels fundamentally different from previous AI cycles.
These systems are no longer passive copilots.
They execute, coordinate, persist state, navigate codebases, run tools, and increasingly operate asynchronously.
The stack is quietly shifting from:
“humans using tools”
to
“agents operating environments.”
And once that happens, optimization moves upward into orchestration, memory, governance, and skill layers.
The models are just the runtime.
Portable Operational Intelligence
The paper also hints at something deeper:
Skill optimization transfers surprisingly well across models and environments.
That suggests we’re starting to discover reusable abstractions for agent behavior itself — not just prompts or fine-tuning, but portable operational intelligence.
That’s a huge deal.
Because the winning AI-native companies may not be the ones with the biggest models.
They may be the ones with the best context systems, orchestration layers, memory architectures, and evolving agent skill ecosystems.
That’s where I think this is heading."}
